topic_name: String DP — LCS, Edit Distance, Palindromic Subsequence

exercises.title: What is the Longest Common Subsequence?
exercises.instructions:
Find the LCS (not substring — characters don't need to be contiguous):
A: s1='abcde', s2='ace' → LCS = ?
B: s1='abc', s2='abc' → LCS = ?
C: s1='abc', s2='def' → LCS = ?
D: s1='RAZORPAY', s2='PAYTM' → LCS = ?
exercises.expected_solution:
A: s1='abcde', s2='ace'
   LCS = 'ace' — length 3
   Subsequence: pick a(0), c(2), e(4) from s1 to match s2
   Characters must appear in ORDER but need not be contiguous

B: s1='abc', s2='abc'
   LCS = 'abc' — length 3 (identical strings)
   The LCS of identical strings is the string itself

C: s1='abc', s2='def'
   LCS = '' — length 0 (no common characters)
   No character in abc appears in def

D: s1='RAZORPAY', s2='PAYTM'
   LCS = 'AY' or 'PY' or 'PAY' — let's verify 'PAY':
   P: R-A-Z-O-R-P(5)-A-Y, P-A-Y-T-M → P matches at s1[5]
   A: after P(5) in s1: A(6), A in PAYTM: A(1) after P(0) → match
   Y: after A(6) in s1: Y(7), Y in PAYTM: Y(2) after A(1) → match
   LCS = 'PAY', length 3

test_cases.expected:
A: s1='abcde', s2='ace'
   LCS = 'ace' — length 3
   Subsequence: pick a(0), c(2), e(4) from s1 to match s2
   Characters must appear in ORDER but need not be contiguous

B: s1='abc', s2='abc'
   LCS = 'abc' — length 3 (identical strings)
   The LCS of identical strings is the string itself

C: s1='abc', s2='def'
   LCS = '' — length 0 (no common characters)
   No character in abc appears in def

D: s1='RAZORPAY', s2='PAYTM'
   LCS = 'AY' or 'PY' or 'PAY' — let's verify 'PAY':
   P: R-A-Z-O-R-P(5)-A-Y, P-A-Y-T-M → P matches at s1[5]
   A: after P(5) in s1: A(6), A in PAYTM: A(1) after P(0) → match
   Y: after A(6) in s1: Y(7), Y in PAYTM: Y(2) after A(1) → match
   LCS = 'PAY', length 3

hints: LCS: characters must appear in order but not contiguous
LCS of identical strings = the string itself
LCS of strings with no common chars = 0

exercises.title: Edit distance — what operations are allowed?
exercises.instructions:
Edit distance (Levenshtein) between 'horse' and 'ros'.
Allowed: insert, delete, replace (each costs 1).

Trace the minimum operations to transform 'horse' → 'ros'.
exercises.expected_solution:
'horse' → 'ros': minimum 3 operations

Step 1: horse → rorse (replace 'h' with 'r')
Step 2: rorse → rose  (delete 'r' at position 2)
Step 3: rose  → ros   (delete 'e')

Total: 3 operations = edit distance of 3

Alternate path (also 3 ops):
Step 1: horse → horse (no-op)
Step 2: horse → hrse  (delete 'o')
Step 3: hrse  → hse   (delete 'r')
... this gets worse

The DP recurrence:
  if s1[i] == s2[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]  (free match)
  else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(
    dp[i-1][j],   // delete from s1
    dp[i][j-1],   // insert into s1 (= delete from s2)
    dp[i-1][j-1]  // replace
  )

Final answer: dp[5][3] = 3

test_cases.expected:
'horse' → 'ros': minimum 3 operations

Step 1: horse → rorse (replace 'h' with 'r')
Step 2: rorse → rose  (delete 'r' at position 2)
Step 3: rose  → ros   (delete 'e')

Total: 3 operations = edit distance of 3

Alternate path (also 3 ops):
Step 1: horse → horse (no-op)
Step 2: horse → hrse  (delete 'o')
Step 3: hrse  → hse   (delete 'r')
... this gets worse

The DP recurrence:
  if s1[i] == s2[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]  (free match)
  else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(
    dp[i-1][j],   // delete from s1
    dp[i][j-1],   // insert into s1 (= delete from s2)
    dp[i-1][j-1]  // replace
  )

Final answer: dp[5][3] = 3

hints: Three operations: insert, delete, replace
Match: dp[i-1][j-1] (free, no cost)
Mismatch: 1 + min(delete, insert, replace)

exercises.title: Longest Common Subsequence (LeetCode #1143)
exercises.instructions:
Find length of LCS of two strings.

text1='abcde', text2='ace' → 3
text1='abc', text2='abc' → 3
text1='abc', text2='def' → 0

O(m×n) time, O(m×n) space — classic DP.
hints: dp[i][j]: LCS of text1[0..i-1] and text2[0..j-1]
Match: dp[i-1][j-1]+1 (extend LCS)
Mismatch: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) — skip one char from either string

exercises.title: Edit Distance (LeetCode #72)
exercises.instructions:
Find minimum operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert word1 to word2.

word1='horse', word2='ros' → 3
word1='intention', word2='execution' → 5

O(m×n) time, O(m×n) space DP.
hints: Base case: dp[i][0]=i (delete i chars), dp[0][j]=j (insert j chars)
Match: dp[i-1][j-1] (free, no cost)
Mismatch: 1 + min(replace=dp[i-1][j-1], delete=dp[i-1][j], insert=dp[i][j-1])

exercises.title: Longest Common Substring (different from subsequence!)
exercises.instructions:
Find length of longest COMMON SUBSTRING (contiguous, not subsequence).

s1='abcde', s2='abfce' → 2 ('ab')
s1='RAZORPAY', s2='REPAY' → 3 ('PAY')

DP: dp[i][j] = length of longest common substring ending at s1[i-1] and s2[j-1].
If mismatch: dp[i][j] = 0 (substring must be contiguous).
hints: Substring: dp[i][j]=0 on mismatch (contiguous requirement)
Track maxLen throughout — answer is not at dp[m][n]
Subsequence vs substring: different DP recurrence

exercises.title: Longest Palindromic Subsequence (LeetCode #516)
exercises.instructions:
Find length of longest palindromic subsequence in s.

s='bbbab' → 4 ('bbbb')
s='cbbd' → 2 ('bb')

Key: LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)).
Alternately: DP where dp[i][j] = LPS length in s[i..j].
hints: LPS = LCS(s, reverse(s)) — palindrome reads same forwards and backwards
Use the standard LCS DP
Can also solve directly: dp[i][j] = LPS in s[i..j]

exercises.title: Minimum Insertions to Make String Palindrome (LeetCode #1312)
exercises.instructions:
Find minimum insertions to make string a palindrome.

s='zzazz' → 0 (already palindrome)
s='mbadm' → 2
s='leetcode' → 5

Key: minInsertions = n - LongestPalindromicSubsequence(s)
The LPS chars form a palindrome — we need to insert n - LPS chars.
hints: LPS chars form the palindrome backbone
Non-LPS chars: each needs one symmetric insertion
minInsertions = n - LPS(s)

exercises.title: Distinct Subsequences (LeetCode #115)
exercises.instructions:
Count distinct ways subsequence of s equals t.

s='rabbbit', t='rabbit' → 3
s='babgbag', t='bag' → 5

dp[i][j] = number of ways t[0..j-1] appears as subsequence in s[0..i-1].
hints: dp[i][0]=1: empty t matches any s prefix exactly once
Skip s[i]: dp[i-1][j] (always)
Use s[i]: add dp[i-1][j-1] only when s[i]==t[j]

exercises.title: Shortest Common Supersequence (LeetCode #1092)
exercises.instructions:
Find the shortest string that has both str1 and str2 as subsequences.

str1='abac', str2='cab' → 'cabac' (length 5)

Key: SCS length = len(str1) + len(str2) - LCS(str1, str2)
Construct: use LCS DP table, then backtrack.
hints: LCS chars appear once in SCS; non-LCS chars from both strings added
Backtrack from dp[m][n]: match → include once, else include from the larger side
Reverse at end: built backwards during backtracking

exercises.title: Space optimization for LCS — rolling array
exercises.instructions:
The standard LCS DP uses O(m×n) space. Show how to reduce to O(n) space using two rows.

Explain:
1. Which cells of the DP table does cell (i,j) depend on?
2. How does this enable the rolling two-row optimization?
3. What's the space reduction for m=1000, n=500?
exercises.expected_solution:
Standard LCS DP dependencies:
  dp[i][j] = f(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
  Only depends on: current row and PREVIOUS row
  Current row: dp[i][j-1] (already computed in this row)
  Previous row: dp[i-1][j] and dp[i-1][j-1]

Rolling array optimization:
  Keep only 2 rows: prev[] and curr[]
  After each outer loop iteration: prev = curr
  Space: O(2n) = O(n) instead of O(m×n)

Java implementation:
  int[] prev = new int[n+1], curr = new int[n+1];
  for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
      for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
          if (s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]) curr[j] = prev[j-1] + 1;
          else curr[j] = Math.max(prev[j], curr[j-1]);
      }
      int[] tmp = prev; prev = curr; curr = tmp; // swap (reuse arrays)
  }
  return prev[n]; // last swap makes 'prev' the final row

Space reduction for m=1000, n=500:
  Standard: 1000 × 500 × 4 bytes = 2,000,000 bytes = 2 MB
  Optimized: 2 × 500 × 4 bytes = 4,000 bytes = 4 KB
  Reduction: 500x less memory!

Note: the rolling array loses the ability to backtrack the actual LCS sequence.
If you need to reconstruct the sequence, you must keep the full O(m×n) table.

test_cases.expected:
Standard LCS DP dependencies:
  dp[i][j] = f(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
  Only depends on: current row and PREVIOUS row
  Current row: dp[i][j-1] (already computed in this row)
  Previous row: dp[i-1][j] and dp[i-1][j-1]

Rolling array optimization:
  Keep only 2 rows: prev[] and curr[]
  After each outer loop iteration: prev = curr
  Space: O(2n) = O(n) instead of O(m×n)

Java implementation:
  int[] prev = new int[n+1], curr = new int[n+1];
  for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
      for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
          if (s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]) curr[j] = prev[j-1] + 1;
          else curr[j] = Math.max(prev[j], curr[j-1]);
      }
      int[] tmp = prev; prev = curr; curr = tmp; // swap (reuse arrays)
  }
  return prev[n]; // last swap makes 'prev' the final row

Space reduction for m=1000, n=500:
  Standard: 1000 × 500 × 4 bytes = 2,000,000 bytes = 2 MB
  Optimized: 2 × 500 × 4 bytes = 4,000 bytes = 4 KB
  Reduction: 500x less memory!

Note: the rolling array loses the ability to backtrack the actual LCS sequence.
If you need to reconstruct the sequence, you must keep the full O(m×n) table.

hints: dp[i][j] depends only on row i-1 and earlier columns of row i
Two arrays: prev (row i-1) and curr (row i)
Swap arrays each iteration rather than copying

exercises.title: Wildcard Matching (LeetCode #44)
exercises.instructions:
Implement wildcard pattern matching with '?' (any single char) and '*' (any sequence including empty).

s='aa', p='a' → false
s='aa', p='*' → true
s='cb', p='?a' → false
s='adceb', p='*a*b' → true

O(m×n) DP.
hints: '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] (match empty) OR dp[i-1][j] (match one more)
'?': matches any single char → dp[i-1][j-1]
Base: dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] if p[j-1]=='*' (star can match empty prefix)

exercises.title: Minimum ASCII Delete Sum (LeetCode #712)
exercises.instructions:
Find minimum sum of ASCII values of deleted characters to make two strings equal.

s1='sea', s2='eat' → 231 (delete 's': 115, delete 't': 116)
s1='delete', s2='leet' → 403

Variant of edit distance — delete cost = ASCII value of char.
hints: Base case: dp[i][0] = sum of ASCII of s1[0..i-1] (must delete all)
Match: dp[i-1][j-1] (keep this char, no cost)
Mismatch: min(delete s1[i], delete s2[j]) — each costs ASCII value

exercises.title: Interleaving Strings (LeetCode #97)
exercises.instructions:
Check if s3 is formed by interleaving s1 and s2 (preserving relative order of both).

s1='aabcc', s2='dbbca', s3='aadbbcbcac' → true
s1='aabcc', s2='dbbca', s3='aadbbbaccc' → false

O(m×n) DP.
hints: s3[i+j-1]: current s3 char when i from s1 and j from s2 used
dp[i][j]: can s3[0..i+j-1] be formed by interleaving s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]
From dp[i-1][j]: use s1[i] last. From dp[i][j-1]: use s2[j] last.

exercises.title: Edit Distance with rollover — O(n) space
exercises.instructions:
Implement Edit Distance with O(n) space (rolling array) instead of O(m×n).

word1='horse', word2='ros' → 3

Show both implementations and verify same result. Print space used.
hints: Rolling: only keep prev and curr rows
curr[0] = i: delete i chars from s1 to match empty s2 prefix
Swap arrays each iteration

exercises.title: Complete M31 T5 String DP Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete String DP toolkit:
1. lcs(s1, s2): O(m×n) LCS length
2. editDistance(s1, s2): O(m×n) Levenshtein
3. longestPalindromeSubseq(s): n - LPS(s)
4. All 4 tests pass + print a pattern summary
hints: LCS match: dp[i-1][j-1]+1. EditDist match: dp[i-1][j-1] (free)
EditDist mismatch: 1+min(3 neighbors: replace, delete, insert)
LPS = LCS(s, reverse(s)) — elegant reduction
